title: Growing rosemary from cuttings
subtitle: Take a semi-ripe cutting in early autumn, root it in a 9 cm pot, plant out the next spring.
excerpt: Rosemary roots easily from a semi-ripe cutting taken between August and October. A 15 cm side-shoot off an established plant, stripped of its lower leaves and pushed into a 9 cm pot of gritty compost, becomes a new plant by spring. Free rosemary plants, every year, from one parent bush.
sourceNotes:
Cultural notes drawn from two references:

• Royal Horticultural Society, rosemary propagation guide (rhs.org.uk/plants/rosemary) for the semi-ripe cutting timing and the rooting medium.
• Mrs Earle, Pot-Pourri from a Surrey Garden (1897), §The herb garden. Project Gutenberg #14443. Rosemary appears in the traditional English cottage herb plot alongside thyme, sage, and lavender.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cutting taken from the current year's growth after it has started to firm up at the base but is still soft at the tip. Mid-summer to early autumn is the usual window. Semi-ripe cuttings root more reliably than soft-tip cuttings and faster than hardwood cuttings.
A point along a stem where a leaf or pair of leaves grows. Roots form most easily at a node, so cuttings are trimmed with a sharp cut just below a node.
Moving a young plant from its initial small pot to a larger one once the roots have filled the first pot. Done at the right stage: roots showing at the drainage holes but the pot not yet rootbound.

content.text: Rosemary (Salvia rosmarinus, formerly Rosmarinus officinalis) is the easiest of the woody Mediterranean herbs to propagate. One established plant produces dozens of suitable cuttings each year; each cutting becomes a fresh plant by the following spring with very little work. A row of new rosemary plants for the cost of nothing but a few pots and some compost.
The timing matters more than the method. Take cuttings between August and October when the year's new growth has firmed up at the base but is still soft at the tip. This is what gardeners call a
semi-ripe cutting
. Earlier and the cutting is too soft and rots in the pot; later and it's too woody and slow to root.
What you need

An established rosemary plant with healthy current-year growth to take cuttings from. Sharp secateurs or a sharp knife. Six to ten 9 cm plastic pots with drainage holes (or one large seed tray). A bag of gritty cutting compost (mix one part horticultural grit or perlite into two parts ordinary multipurpose compost). A windowsill, cold frame, or sheltered porch out of direct sunlight for the first six weeks.
Taking the cuttings
Pick a healthy side-shoot 10 to 15 cm long off the parent plant. The shoot should be from this year's growth, firm at the base where it joins the main stem, still soft at the tip.
Snip the shoot from the parent plant with a clean cut. Don't pull or twist; the base needs to be clean.
Trim the cutting to about 10 cm, cutting cleanly just below a leaf
node
. This bottom cut is where new roots will form.
Strip the lower two-thirds of leaves off the cutting, leaving only the top few pairs. Buried leaves rot; exposed leaves photosynthesise and keep the cutting alive while roots form.
Optional: dip the cut base in rooting hormone powder. Rosemary roots without it but rooting hormone speeds the process by a week or two.
Push the cutting base 3 cm into a pot of gritty compost. Three cuttings to a 9 cm pot is the standard density; insert them around the edge of the pot where the rooting compost is best aerated.
Water the pot from below by standing it in a tray of water for ten minutes, then drain.
Rooting through autumn and winter

Stand the pots on a sheltered windowsill, in a cold frame, or in an unheated porch. Don't put them in full sun (the cuttings desiccate before they root). A bright but cool spot at 12 to 16 °C is ideal.

Water sparingly through autumn. The compost should be just damp, never wet. Overwatering is the single biggest cause of cutting failure: the bases rot before roots form. Check every 7 to 10 days and water from below if the surface is dry.

Roots form over 6 to 10 weeks. The first sign of success is new green growth at the tip of the cutting; this means the roots have established and the cutting is feeding itself. Tugging gently on the cutting at this stage will meet resistance from the new roots.
Potting on in spring
By March or April the rooted cuttings are ready for
potting on

. Ease them out of the 9 cm pots (the rooted plants come out as a clump of three or four; gently separate them) and pot each into its own 12 cm or 1 litre pot of ordinary multipurpose compost. Water in.

Grow on outdoors in a sheltered spot for another couple of months. By May or June the young plants are ready to plant out into their final positions: in a herb bed, a sunny border, a large pot. Rosemary likes full sun and well-drained soil; it dislikes heavy wet clay.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong with rosemary cuttings
attrs.intro: Four failure modes to know about.

items.symptom: Cuttings go black at the base within two weeks
items.cause: Overwatering, often combined with a sealed-in propagator that kept the air too humid.
items.fix: Don't use a propagator lid for rosemary. The cuttings prefer airflow. Water from below only when the compost surface is dry. Discard the affected pots and try again with the next set of cuttings.

items.symptom: All the leaves have dropped off the cuttings
items.cause: The cuttings have desiccated, usually from direct sunlight or a dry indoor radiator nearby.
items.fix: Move to a cooler, brighter spot away from direct sun and any heat source. Cuttings that still have a firm green stem can recover; cuttings whose stems have shrivelled won't.

items.symptom: Cuttings sit unchanged for three months with no new growth
items.cause: Either the cuttings were taken too late (woody hardwood instead of semi-ripe) or the pot is too cold. Hardwood cuttings root eventually but slowly.
items.fix: Be patient and wait until spring; many will root once temperatures rise. If by May there's still no growth, the cuttings have failed; start over with fresh semi-ripe wood from the parent plant in August.

items.symptom: Rooted cuttings die in their first winter outdoors
items.cause: Young rosemary roots are not yet hardy enough for a wet British winter in the ground. Container plants on heavy clay sometimes drown.
items.fix: Plant out young rosemary in late spring, not autumn. Add grit to heavy soil to improve drainage. Container plants survive winter better if moved to a wall-sheltered spot and raised on pot feet to drain freely.

content.text: Where this goes next

The technique transfers cleanly to other woody Mediterranean herbs: thyme, sage, lavender, and bay all root from semi-ripe cuttings in the same August-to-October window. Once one plant of each is established in the garden, you never need to buy these herbs again. A row of homemade rosemary plants also makes generous gifts to neighbouring gardeners through the spring.
